context.pr_title: Fix for failing test_site_module_disabled on macOS and python 3.9
context.pr_body:
Over the last few days, the `test_site_module_disabled` started to fail on macOS python 3.9 runner (e.g., [here](https://github.com/pyinstaller/pyinstaller/actions/runs/14001775971/job/39212391324?pr=9067)).

The failure is caused by `site` module being already imported at the start of the test, which is explicitly checking that this is not the case (as `site` module should be disabled in the interpreter config, and thus not imported during its initialization). 

It turns out that this is caused by import of `setuptools` in the run-time hook for `setuptools`, but only with `setuptools` v77.0.3 that was released on Mar 20. The import chain is `pyi_rth_setuptools.py` -> `setuptools` -> `setuptools.dist` -> `setuptools.command` -> `setuptools._distutils.command` -> `setuptools._distutils.command.build_ext` -> `site`,
and was likely brought about by https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/commit/1d120d919bd6d5cb8ba1b179c2099bd947d2c466.

It also turns out that the failing test under python 3.9 on macOS is collecting `setuptools`, while the (succeeding) tests under other versions (and OSes) are not. The offending import chain turns out to be: `pyi_site_module_disabled.py` -> `site` -> `_sitebuiltins` -> `pydoc` -> `sysconfig` -> `_osx_support` -> `distutils` (-> `setuptools`).

This is caused by a [conditional import in `_osx_support.compiler_fixup`](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/f3994ade31a563d49806cf6a681d1b1115fccaa3/Lib/_osx_support.py#L430-L434), which itself seems to be intended to be used by `distutils`.

This explains why we are seeing this only on macOS, and only with python 3.9 - latest `setuptools` do not  support python 3.8 anymore, and the use of `distutils.log` in `_osx_support.compiler_fixup` was removed in python >= 3.10.

Therefore, add a hook for `_osx_support` that excludes `distutils`. In addition to fixing the failing test, this should also ensure that `distutils` / `setuptools` do no end up pulled into basic applications that import either `site` or `sysconfig` (or a module that imports either of these) when building with python 3.8 or 3.9 on macOs.

agents.input_prompt:
Analyze this Pull Request:

## Title
Fix for failing test_site_module_disabled on macOS and python 3.9

## Description
Over the last few days, the `test_site_module_disabled` started to fail on macOS python 3.9 runner (e.g., [here](https://github.com/pyinstaller/pyinstaller/actions/runs/14001775971/job/39212391324?pr=9067)).

The failure is caused by `site` module being already imported at the start of the test, which is explicitly checking that this is not the case (as `site` module should be disabled in the interpreter config, and thus not imported during its initialization). 

It turns out that this is caused by import of `setuptools` in the run-time hook for `setuptools`, but only with `setuptools` v77.0.3 that was released on Mar 20. The import chain is `pyi_rth_setuptools.py` -> `setuptools` -> `setuptools.dist` -> `setuptools.command` -> `setuptools._distutils.command` -> `setuptools._distutils.command.build_ext` -> `site`,
and was likely brought about by https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/commit/1d120d919bd6d5cb8ba1b179c2099bd947d2c466.

It also turns out that the failing test under python 3.9 on macOS is collecting `setuptools`, while the (succeeding) tests under other versions (and OSes) are not. The offending import chain turns out to be: `pyi_site_module_disabled.py` -> `site` -> `_sitebuiltins` -> `pydoc` -> `sysconfig` -> `_osx_support` -> `distutils` (-> `setuptools`).

This is caused by a [conditional import in `_osx_support.compiler_fixup`](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/f3994ade31a563d49806cf6a681d1b1115fccaa3/Lib/_osx_support.py#L430-L434), which itself seems to be intended to be used by `distutils`.

This explains why we are seeing this only on macOS, and only with python 3.9 - latest `setuptools` do not  support python 3.8 anymore, and the use of `distutils.log` in `_osx_support.compiler_fixup` was removed in python >= 3.10.

Therefore, add a hook for `_osx_support` that excludes `distutils`. In addition to fixing the failing test, this should also ensure that `distutils` / `setuptools` do no end up pulled into basic applications that import either `site` or `sysconfig` (or a module that imports either of these) when building with python 3.8 or 3.9 on macOs.

Explore the source code to identify the files and functions to modify.

# This runtime hook performs the equivalent of the distutils-precedence.pth from the setuptools package;
# it registers a special meta finder that diverts import of distutils to setuptools._distutils, if available.


def _pyi_rthook():
    def _install_setuptools_distutils_hack():
        import os
        import setuptools

        # We need to query setuptools version at runtime, because the default value for SETUPTOOLS_USE_DISTUTILS
        # has changed at version 60.0 from "stdlib" to "local", and we want to mimic that behavior.
        setuptools_major = int(setuptools.__version__.split('.')[0])
        default_value = "stdlib" if setuptools_major < 60 else "local"

        if os.environ.get("SETUPTOOLS_USE_DISTUTILS", default_value) == "local":
            import _distutils_hack

from PyInstaller.utils.hooks.setuptools import setuptools_info

hiddenimports = []

# From Python 3.6 and later ``distutils.sysconfig`` takes on the same behaviour as regular ``sysconfig`` of moving the
# config vars to a module (see hook-sysconfig.py). It doesn't use a nice `get module name` function like ``sysconfig``
# does to help us locate it but the module is the same file that ``sysconfig`` uses so we can use the
# ``_get_sysconfigdata_name()`` from regular ``sysconfig``.
try:
    import sysconfig
    hiddenimports += [sysconfig._get_sysconfigdata_name()]
except AttributeError:
    # Either sysconfig has no attribute _get_sysconfigdata_name (i.e., the function does not exist), or this is Windows
    # and the _get_sysconfigdata_name() call failed due to missing sys.abiflags attribute.
    pass

# Starting with setuptools 60.0, the vendored distutils overrides the stdlib one (which will be removed in python 3.12
# anyway), so check if we are using that version. While the distutils override behavior can be controleld via the
# ``SETUPTOOLS_USE_DISTUTILS`` environment variable, the latter may have a different value during the build and at the
# runtime, and so we need to ensure that both stdlib and setuptools variant of distutils are collected.
if setuptools_info.available and setuptools_info.version >= (60, 0):
    hiddenimports += ['setuptools._distutils']

hiddenimports = []

# From Python 3.6 and later ``distutils.sysconfig`` takes on the same behaviour as regular ``sysconfig`` of moving the
# config vars to a module (see hook-sysconfig.py). It doesn't use a nice `get module name` function like ``sysconfig``

# ``SETUPTOOLS_USE_DISTUTILS`` environment variable, the latter may have a different value during the build and at the
# runtime, and so we need to ensure that both stdlib and setuptools variant of distutils are collected.
if setuptools_info.available and setuptools_info.version >= (60, 0):

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Copyright (c) 2024, PyInstaller Development Team.
#
# Distributed under the terms of the GNU General Public License (version 2
# or later) with exception for distributing the bootloader.
#
# The full license is in the file COPYING.txt, distributed with this software.

# SPDX-License-Identifier: (GPL-2.0-or-later WITH Bootloader-exception)
#-----------------------------------------------------------------------------
from PyInstaller import log as logging
from PyInstaller import isolated

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)


# Import setuptools and analyze its properties in an isolated subprocess. This function is called by `SetuptoolsInfo`
# to initialize its properties.
@isolated.decorate
def _retrieve_setuptools_info():
    import importlib

    try:
        setuptools = importlib.import_module("setuptools")  # noqa: F841
    except ModuleNotFoundError:
        return None

    # Delay these imports until after we have confirmed that setuptools is importable.
    import pathlib

    import packaging.version

    from PyInstaller.compat import importlib_metadata
    from PyInstaller.utils.hooks import (
        collect_data_files,
        collect_submodules,
    )

    # Try to retrieve the version. At this point, failure is consider an error.
    version_string = importlib_metadata.version("setuptools")
    version = packaging.version.Version(version_string).release  # Use the version tuple

    # setuptools >= 60.0 its vendored copy of distutils (mainly due to its removal from stdlib in python >= 3.12).
    distutils_vendored = False
    distutils_modules = []
    if version >= (60, 0):
        distutils_vendored = True
        distutils_modules += ["_distutils_hack"]
        distutils_modules += collect_submodules(
            "setuptools._distutils",
            # setuptools 71.0.1 ~ 71.0.4 include `setuptools._distutils.tests`; avoid explicitly collecting it
            # (t was not included in earlier setuptools releases).
            filter=lambda name: name != 'setuptools._distutils.tests',
        )

    # Check if `setuptools._vendor` exists. Some linux distributions opt to de-vendor `setuptools` and remove the
    # `setuptools._vendor` directory altogether. If this is the case, most of additional processing below should be
    # skipped to avoid errors and warnings about non-existent `setuptools._vendor` module.
    try:
        setuptools_vendor = importlib.import_module("setuptools._vendor")
    except ModuleNotFoundError:
        setuptools_vendor = None

    # Check for exposed packages/modules that are vendored by setuptools. If stand-alone version is not provided in the
    # environment, setuptools-vendored version is exposed (due to location of `setuptools._vendor` being appended to
    # `sys.path`. Applicable to v71.0.0 and later.
    vendored_status = dict()
    if version >= (71, 0) and setuptools_vendor is not None:
        VENDORED_CANDIDATES = (
            "autocommand",
            "backports.tarfile",
            "importlib_metadata",
            "importlib_resources",
            "inflect",
            "jaraco.context",
            "jaraco.functools",
            "jaraco.text",
            "more_itertools",
            "ordered_set",
            "packaging",
            "platformdirs",
            "tomli",
            "typeguard",
            "typing_extensions",
            "wheel",
            "zipp",
        )

        # Resolve path(s) of `setuptools_vendor` package.
        setuptools_vendor_paths = [pathlib.Path(path).resolve() for path in setuptools_vendor.__path__]

        # Process each candidate
        for candidate_name in VENDORED_CANDIDATES:
            try:
                candidate = importlib.import_module(candidate_name)
            except ImportError:
                continue

            # Check the __file__ attribute (modules and regular packages). Will not work with namespace packages, but
            # at the moment, there are none.
            candidate_file_attr = getattr(candidate, '__file__', None)
            if candidate_file_attr is not None:
                candidate_path = pathlib.Path(candidate_file_attr).parent.resolve()
                is_vendored = any([
                    setuptools_vendor_path in candidate_path.parents
                    for setuptools_vendor_path in setuptools_vendor_paths
                ])
                vendored_status[candidate_name] = is_vendored

    # Collect submodules from `setuptools._vendor`, regardless of whether the vendored package is exposed or
    # not (because setuptools might need/use it either way).
    vendored_modules = []
    if setuptools_vendor is not None:
        EXCLUDED_VENDORED_MODULES = (
            # Prevent recursing into setuptools._vendor.pyparsing.diagram, which typically fails to be imported due
            # to missing dependencies (railroad, pyparsing (?), jinja2) and generates a warning... As the module is
            # usually unimportable, it is likely not to be used by setuptools. NOTE: pyparsing was removed from
            # vendored packages in setuptools v67.0.0; keep this exclude around for earlier versions.
            'setuptools._vendor.pyparsing.diagram',
            # Setuptools >= 71 started shipping vendored dependencies that include tests; avoid collecting those via
            # hidden imports. (Note that this also prevents creation of aliases for these module, but that should
            # not be an issue, as they should not be referenced from anywhere).
            'setuptools._vendor.importlib_resources.tests',
            # These appear to be utility scripts bundled with the jaraco.text package - exclude them.
            'setuptools._vendor.jaraco.text.show-newlines',
            'setuptools._vendor.jaraco.text.strip-prefix',
            'setuptools._vendor.jaraco.text.to-dvorak',
            'setuptools._vendor.jaraco.text.to-qwerty',
        )
        vendored_modules += collect_submodules(
            'setuptools._vendor',
            filter=lambda name: name not in EXCLUDED_VENDORED_MODULES,
        )

        # `collect_submodules` (and its underlying `pkgutil.iter_modules` do not discover namespace sub-packages, in
        # this case `setuptools._vendor.jaraco`. So force a manual scan of modules/packages inside it.
        vendored_modules += collect_submodules(
            'setuptools._vendor.jaraco',
            filter=lambda name: name not in EXCLUDED_VENDORED_MODULES,
        )

    # *** Data files for vendored packages ***
    vendored_data = []

    if version >= (71, 0) and setuptools_vendor is not None:
        # Since the vendored dependencies from `setuptools/_vendor` are now visible to the outside world, make
        # sure we collect their metadata. (We cannot use copy_metadata here, because we need to collect data
        # files to their original locations).
        vendored_data += collect_data_files('setuptools._vendor', includes=['**/*.dist-info'])
        # Similarly, ensure that `Lorem ipsum.txt` from vendored jaraco.text is collected
        vendored_data += collect_data_files('setuptools._vendor.jaraco.text', includes=['**/Lorem ipsum.txt'])

    # Return dictionary with collected information
    return {
        "available": True,
        "version": version,
        "distutils_vendored": distutils_vendored,
        "distutils_modules": distutils_modules,
        "vendored_status": vendored_status,
        "vendored_modules": vendored_modules,
        "vendored_data": vendored_data,
    }


class SetuptoolsInfo:
    def __init__(self):
        pass

    def __repr__(self):
        return "SetuptoolsInfo"

    # Delay initialization of setuptools information until until the corresponding attributes are first requested.
    def __getattr__(self, name):
        if 'available' in self.__dict__:
            # Initialization was already done, but requested attribute is not available.
            raise AttributeError(name)

        # Load setuptools info...
        self._load_setuptools_info()
        # ... and return the requested attribute
        return getattr(self, name)

    def _load_setuptools_info(self):
        logger.info("%s: initializing cached setuptools info...", self)

        # Initialize variables so that they might be accessed even if setuptools is unavailable or if initialization
        # fails for some reason.
        self.available = False
        self.version = None
        self.distutils_vendored = False
        self.distutils_modules = []
        self.vendored_status = dict()
        self.vendored_modules = []
        self.vendored_data = []

        try:
            setuptools_info = _retrieve_setuptools_info()
        except Exception as e:
            logger.warning("%s: failed to obtain setuptools info: %s", self, e)
            return

        # If package could not be imported, `_retrieve_setuptools_info` returns None. In such cases, emit a debug
        # message instead of a warning, because this initialization might be triggered by a helper function that is
        # trying to determine availability of `setuptools` by inspecting the `available` attribute.
        if setuptools_info is None:
            logger.debug("%s: failed to obtain setuptools info: setuptools could not be imported.", self)
            return

        # Copy properties
        for key, value in setuptools_info.items():
            setattr(self, key, value)

    def is_vendored(self, module_name):
        return self.vendored_status.get(module_name, False)

    @staticmethod
    def _create_vendored_aliases(vendored_name, module_name, modules_list):
        # Create aliases for all submodules
        prefix_len = len(vendored_name)  # Length of target-name prefix to remove
        return ((module_name + vendored_module[prefix_len:], vendored_module) for vendored_module in modules_list
                if vendored_module.startswith(vendored_name))

    def get_vendored_aliases(self, module_name):
        vendored_name = f"setuptools._vendor.{module_name}"
        return self._create_vendored_aliases(vendored_name, module_name, self.vendored_modules)

    def get_distutils_aliases(self):
        vendored_name = "setuptools._distutils"
        return self._create_vendored_aliases(vendored_name, "distutils", self.distutils_modules)


setuptools_info = SetuptoolsInfo()


def pre_safe_import_module(api):
    """
    A common implementation of pre_safe_import_module hook function.

    This function can be either called from the `pre_safe_import_module` function in a pre-safe-import-module hook, or
    just imported into the hook.
    """
    module_name = api.module_name

    # Check if the package/module is a vendored copy. This also returns False is setuptools is unavailable, because
    # vendored module status dictionary will be empty.
    if not setuptools_info.is_vendored(module_name):
        return

    vendored_name = f"setuptools._vendor.{module_name}"
    logger.info(
        "Setuptools: %r appears to be a setuptools-vendored copy - creating alias to %r!", module_name, vendored_name
    )

    # Create aliases for all (sub)modules
    for aliased_name, real_vendored_name in setuptools_info.get_vendored_aliases(module_name):
        api.add_alias_module(real_vendored_name, aliased_name)
tool_calls.result:
### File Overview
This file detects and caches information about the installed setuptools package (version, whether setuptools vendors distutils or other packages, lists of vendored modules and vendored metadata) and exposes a lazy-loading API (the SetuptoolsInfo class and the module-level setuptools_info instance) for hooks to decide how to treat vendored copies. It also provides a helper pre_safe_import_module(api) used by pre-safe-import-module hooks to create alias modules for setuptools-vendored packages.

### Architecture
- Heavy-weight inspection of the installed setuptools package is performed in an isolated subprocess by the function `_retrieve_setuptools_info()` (decorated with `@isolated.decorate`) so that importing/inspecting setuptools does not affect the main process environment.
- The `SetuptoolsInfo` class implements a lazy/cached view of that information: accessing any attribute on the module-level `setuptools_info` instance triggers `_load_setuptools_info()` which calls `_retrieve_setuptools_info()` once and stores the returned properties as attributes on the instance.
- Helper methods on `SetuptoolsInfo` (`is_vendored`, `get_vendored_aliases`, `get_distutils_aliases`) and the function `pre_safe_import_module(api)` are the main integration points for hooks: they allow hooks to detect vendored copies and create alias modules so PyInstaller treats vendored packages as if they were top-level modules.

### Functions & Classes
- `_retrieve_setuptools_info` — module-level function, decorated with `@isolated.decorate`.
  - Purpose: Run inside an isolated subprocess to safely import and inspect `setuptools` and related packages without impacting the main process.
  - What it does / returned data:
    - Attempts `importlib.import_module("setuptools")`; returns `None` if not importable.
    - Uses `importlib_metadata.version("setuptools")` and `packaging.version.Version` to determine `version` as a tuple (the `.release` attribute from packaging.version.Version).
    - Determines `distutils_vendored` (bool) and collects `distutils_modules` when setuptools version >= (60, 0) by collecting submodules under `setuptools._distutils`.
    - Detects whether certain candidate packages are exposed via `setuptools._vendor` and sets entries in `vendored_status` (a dict mapping candidate package names to bool).
    - Collects `vendored_modules` by using `collect_submodules` on `setuptools._vendor` and `setuptools._vendor.jaraco` (with exclusions).
    - Collects `vendored_data` (data files) for vendored dependencies via `collect_data_files` when setuptools >= (71, 0).
    - Returns a dict with keys exactly:
      - "available" (True if setuptools import succeeded),
      - "version" (tuple from packaging.version.Version.release),
      - "distutils_vendored" (bool),
      - "distutils_modules" (list),
      - "vendored_status" (dict),
      - "vendored_modules" (list),
      - "vendored_data" (list).
  - Importance: This is the single place that analyzes the environment and produces the authoritative data that `SetuptoolsInfo` caches.

- `class SetuptoolsInfo` — class that lazily caches setuptools inspection data.
  - `__init__(self)` — constructor.
    - Currently empty (pass). Instance created at module import as `setuptools_info`.
  - `__repr__(self)` — returns the string "SetuptoolsInfo".
    - Makes logging messages readable.
  - `__getattr__(self, name)` — custom attribute access hook.
    - Purpose: Delay initialization until a property is first requested. If `available` already in `self.__dict__` and attribute not found, raise AttributeError. Otherwise call `_load_setuptools_info()` and then return the requested attribute via `getattr(self, name)`.
    - Importance: Enables lazy/cached initialization so hooks can cheaply import `setuptools_info` and only trigger the heavy subprocess inspection when they actually need attributes.
  - `_load_setuptools_info(self)` — performs initialization and caching.
    - Purpose: Calls `_retrieve_setuptools_info()` (in isolated subprocess) and copies returned values into attributes on the instance.
    - Initializes default safe attributes first:
      - `self.available = False`
      - `self.version = None`
      - `self.distutils_vendored = False`
      - `self.distutils_modules = []`
      - `self.vendored_status = dict()`
      - `self.vendored_modules = []`
      - `self.vendored_data = []`
    - If `_retrieve_setuptools_info()` raises or returns None, initialization stops (keeps defaults). Otherwise, copies keys from the returned dict to attributes on `self` (so the attribute names match the dict keys returned by `_retrieve_setuptools_info()`).
    - Importance: Ensures attributes exist even if setuptools isn't available or an error occurs, and caches the real values for subsequent access.
  - `is_vendored(self, module_name)` — returns `self.vendored_status.get(module_name, False)`.
    - Purpose: Check whether `module_name` (a top-level package name, e.g., "importlib_metadata" or "tomli") is a setuptools-vendored copy.
    - Importance: Primary convenience method used by hooks to detect vendored modules.
  - `_create_vendored_aliases(vendored_name, module_name, modules_list)` — `@staticmethod`.
    - Purpose: Given a vendored prefix (e.g., "setuptools._vendor.packagename"), a target alias base (e.g., "packagename" or "distutils"), and a list of discovered vendored module names (`modules_list`), create pairs (alias_name, vendored_module_name) for every vendored submodule whose name starts with `vendored_name`. The alias_name is constructed by replacing the vendored prefix with the desired module_name prefix.
    - Returns a generator of (alias_name, vendored_module_name) pairs.
    - Importance: Utility to create alias mappings used by get_vendored_aliases and get_distutils_aliases.
  - `get_vendored_aliases(self, module_name)` — returns aliases for a vendored package.
    - Purpose: For `module_name` (top-level name, e.g. "tomli"), constructs `vendored_name = f"setuptools._vendor.{module_name}"` and returns generator from `_create_vendored_aliases(vendored_name, module_name, self.vendored_modules)`.
    - Importance: Used by hooks (and specifically by `pre_safe_import_module`) to find which alias -> real vendored-module mappings should be created.
  - `get_distutils_aliases(self)` — returns aliases for distutils when setuptools ships a vendored copy.
    - Purpose: Uses `vendored_name = "setuptools._distutils"` and `_create_vendored_aliases(vendored_name, "distutils", self.distutils_modules)`.
    - Importance: Provides alias mappings so code importing "distutils.*" can be redirected to "setuptools._distutils.*" when setuptools vendors distutils (setuptools >= 60 behavior).
- `setuptools_info = SetuptoolsInfo()` — module-level instance.
  - Purpose: Public, lazily-initialized object that hooks import and read. Any attribute access triggers loading if not already loaded.
  - Importance: Primary entry point for other hooks and logic; the object documented in this file.

- `pre_safe_import_module(api)` — module-level function.
  - Purpose: Helper implementation intended for use by a `pre_safe_import_module` hook. ## File: PyInstaller/utils/hooks/setuptools.py

## Agent's reason for reading this file:
Check setuptools_info used in hooks

## What the agent expects to find:
What is setuptools_info, its attributes, and how to use it

## Source Code:
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Copyright (c) 2024, PyInstaller Development Team.
#
# Distributed under the terms of the GNU General Public License (version 2
# or later) with exception for distributing the bootloader.
#
# The full license is in the file COPYING.txt, distributed with this software.
#
# SPDX-License-Identifier: (GPL-2.0-or-later WITH Bootloader-exception)
#-----------------------------------------------------------------------------
from PyInstaller import log as logging
from PyInstaller import isolated

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)


# Import setuptools and analyze its properties in an isolated subprocess. This function is called by `SetuptoolsInfo`
# to initialize its properties.
@isolated.decorate
def _retrieve_setuptools_info():
    import importlib

    try:
        setuptools = importlib.import_module("setuptools")  # noqa: F841
    except ModuleNotFoundError:
        return None

    # Delay these imports until after we have confirmed that setuptools is importable.
    import pathlib

    import packaging.version

    from PyInstaller.compat import importlib_metadata
    from PyInstaller.utils.hooks import (
        collect_data_files,
        collect_submodules,
    )

    # Try to retrieve the version. At this point, failure is consider an error.
    version_string = importlib_metadata.version("setuptools")
    version = packaging.version.Version(version_string).release  # Use the version tuple

    # setuptools >= 60.0 its vendored copy of distutils (mainly due to its removal from stdlib in python >= 3.12).
    distutils_vendored = False
    distutils_modules = []
    if version >= (60, 0):
        distutils_vendored = True
        distutils_modules += ["_distutils_hack"]
        distutils_modules += collect_submodules(
            "setuptools._distutils",
            # setuptools 71.0.1 ~ 71.0.4 include `setuptools._distutils.tests`; avoid explicitly collecting it
            # (t was not included in earlier setuptools releases).
            filter=lambda name: name != 'setuptools._distutils.tests',
        )

    # Check if `setuptools._vendor` exists. Some linux distributions opt to de-vendor `setuptools` and remove the
    # `setuptools._vendor` directory altogether. If this is the case, most of additional processing below should be
    # skipped to avoid errors and warnings about non-existent `setuptools._vendor` module.
    try:
        setuptools_vendor = importlib.import_module("setuptools._vendor")
    except ModuleNotFoundError:
        setuptools_vendor = None

    # Check for exposed packages/modules that are vendored by setuptools. If stand-alone version is not provided in the
    # environment, setuptools-vendored version is exposed (due to location of `setuptools._vendor` being appended to
    # `sys.path`. Applicable to v71.0.0 and later.
    vendored_status = dict()
    if version >= (71, 0) and setuptools_vendor is not None:
        VENDORED_CANDIDATES = (
            "autocommand",
            "backports.tarfile",
            "importlib_metadata",
            "importlib_resources",
            "inflect",
            "jaraco.context",
            "jaraco.functools",
            "jaraco.text",
            "more_itertools",
            "ordered_set",
            "packaging",
            "platformdirs",
            "tomli",
            "typeguard",
            "typing_extensions",
            "wheel",
            "zipp",
        )

        # Resolve path(s) of `setuptools_vendor` package.
        setuptools_vendor_paths = [pathlib.Path(path).resolve() for path in setuptools_vendor.__path__]

        # Process each candidate
        for candidate_name in VENDORED_CANDIDATES:
            try:
                candidate = importlib.import_module(candidate_name)
            except ImportError:
                continue

            # Check the __file__ attribute (modules and regular packages). Will not work with namespace packages, but
            # at the moment, there are none.
            candidate_file_attr = getattr(candidate, '__file__', None)
            if candidate_file_attr is not None:
                candidate_path = pathlib.Path(candidate_file_attr).parent.resolve()
                is_vendored = any([
                    setuptools_vendor_path in candidate_path.parents
                    for setuptools_vendor_path in setuptools_vendor_paths
                ])
                vendored_status[candidate_name] = is_vendored

    # Collect submodules from `setuptools._vendor`, regardless of whether the vendored package is exposed or
    # not (because setuptools might need/use it either way).
    vendored_modules = []
    if setuptools_vendor is not None:
        EXCLUDED_VENDORED_MODULES = (
            # Prevent recursing into setuptools._vendor.pyparsing.diagram, which typically fails to be imported due
            # to missing dependencies (railroad, pyparsing (?), jinja2) and generates a warning... As the module is
            # usually unimportable, it is likely not to be used by setuptools. NOTE: pyparsing was removed from
            # vendored packages in setuptools v67.0.0; keep this exclude around for earlier versions.
            'setuptools._vendor.pyparsing.diagram',
            # Setuptools >= 71 started shipping vendored dependencies that include tests; avoid collecting those via
            # hidden imports. (Note that this also prevents creation of aliases for these module, but that should
            # not be an issue, as they should not be referenced from anywhere).
            'setuptools._vendor.importlib_resources.tests',
            # These appear to be utility scripts bundled with the jaraco.text package - exclude them.
            'setuptools._vendor.jaraco.text.show-newlines',
            'setuptools._vendor.jaraco.text.strip-prefix',
            'setuptools._vendor.jaraco.text.to-dvorak',
            'setuptools._vendor.jaraco.text.to-qwerty',
        )
        vendored_modules += collect_submodules(
            'setuptools._vendor',
            filter=lambda name: name not in EXCLUDED_VENDORED_MODULES,
        )

        # `collect_submodules` (and its underlying `pkgutil.iter_modules` do not discover namespace sub-packages, in
        # this case `setuptools._vendor.jaraco`. So force a manual scan of modules/packages inside it.
        vendored_modules += collect_submodules(
            'setuptools._vendor.jaraco',
            filter=lambda name: name not in EXCLUDED_VENDORED_MODULES,
        )

    # *** Data files for vendored packages ***
    vendored_data = []

    if version >= (71, 0) and setuptools_vendor is not None:
        # Since the vendored dependencies from `setuptools/_vendor` are now visible to the outside world, make
        # sure we collect their metadata. (We cannot use copy_metadata here, because we need to collect data
        # files to their original locations).
        vendored_data += collect_data_files('setuptools._vendor', includes=['**/*.dist-info'])
        # Similarly, ensure that `Lorem ipsum.txt` from vendored jaraco.text is collected
        vendored_data += collect_data_files('setuptools._vendor.jaraco.text', includes=['**/Lorem ipsum.txt'])

    # Return dictionary with collected information
    return {
        "available": True,
        "version": version,
        "distutils_vendored": distutils_vendored,
        "distutils_modules": distutils_modules,
        "vendored_status": vendored_status,
        "vendored_modules": vendored_modules,
        "vendored_data": vendored_data,
    }


class SetuptoolsInfo:
    def __init__(self):
        pass

    def __repr__(self):
        return "SetuptoolsInfo"

    # Delay initialization of setuptools information until until the corresponding attributes are first requested.
    def __getattr__(self, name):
        if 'available' in self.__dict__:
            # Initialization was already done, but requested attribute is not available.
            raise AttributeError(name)

        # Load setuptools info...
        self._load_setuptools_info()
        # ... and return the requested attribute
        return getattr(self, name)

    def _load_setuptools_info(self):
        logger.info("%s: initializing cached setuptools info...", self)

        # Initialize variables so that they might be accessed even if setuptools is unavailable or if initialization
        # fails for some reason.
        self.available = False
        self.version = None
        self.distutils_vendored = False
        self.distutils_modules = []
        self.vendored_status = dict()
        self.vendored_modules = []
        self.vendored_data = []

        try:
            setuptools_info = _retrieve_setuptools_info()
        except Exception as e:
            logger.warning("%s: failed to obtain setuptools info: %s", self, e)
            return

        # If package could not be imported, `_retrieve_setuptools_info` returns None. In such cases, emit a debug
        # message instead of a warning, because this initialization might be triggered by a helper function that is
        # trying to determine availability of `setuptools` by inspecting the `available` attribute.
        if setuptools_info is None:
            logger.debug("%s: failed to obtain setuptools info: setuptools could not be imported.", self)
            return

        # Copy properties
        for key, value in setuptools_info.items():
            setattr(self, key, value)

    def is_vendored(self, module_name):
        return self.vendored_status.get(module_name, False)

    @staticmethod
    def _create_vendored_aliases(vendored_name, module_name, modules_list):
        # Create aliases for all submodules
        prefix_len = len(vendored_name)  # Length of target-name prefix to remove
        return ((module_name + vendored_module[prefix_len:], vendored_module) for vendored_module in modules_list
                if vendored_module.startswith(vendored_name))

    def get_vendored_aliases(self, module_name):
        vendored_name = f"setuptools._vendor.{module_name}"
        return self._create_vendored_aliases(vendored_name, module_name, self.vendored_modules)

    def get_distutils_aliases(self):
        vendored_name = "setuptools._distutils"
        return self._create_vendored_aliases(vendored_name, "distutils", self.distutils_modules)


setuptools_info = SetuptoolsInfo()


def pre_safe_import_module(api):
    """
    A common implementation of pre_safe_import_module hook function.

    This function can be either called from the `pre_safe_import_module` function in a pre-safe-import-module hook, or
    just imported into the hook.
    """
    module_name = api.module_name

    # Check if the package/module is a vendored copy. This also returns False is setuptools is unavailable, because
    # vendored module status dictionary will be empty.
    if not setuptools_info.is_vendored(module_name):
        return

    vendored_name = f"setuptools._vendor.{module_name}"
    logger.info(
        "Setuptools: %r appears to be a setuptools-vendored copy - creating alias to %r!", module_name, vendored_name
    )

    # Create aliases for all (sub)modules
    for aliased_name, real_vendored_name in setuptools_info.get_vendored_aliases(module_name):
        api.add_alias_module(real_vendored_name, aliased_name)

### File Overview
This file detects and caches information about the installed setuptools package (version, whether setuptools vendors distutils or other packages, lists of vendored modules and vendored metadata) and exposes a lazy-loading API (the SetuptoolsInfo class and the module-level setuptools_info instance) for hooks to decide how to treat vendored copies. It also provides a helper pre_safe_import_module(api) used by pre-safe-import-module hooks to create alias modules for setuptools-vendored packages.

### Architecture
- Heavy-weight inspection of the installed setuptools package is performed in an isolated subprocess by the function `_retrieve_setuptools_info()` (decorated with `@isolated.decorate`) so that importing/inspecting setuptools does not affect the main process environment.
- The `SetuptoolsInfo` class implements a lazy/cached view of that information: accessing any attribute on the module-level `setuptools_info` instance triggers `_load_setuptools_info()` which calls `_retrieve_setuptools_info()` once and stores the returned properties as attributes on the instance.
- Helper methods on `SetuptoolsInfo` (`is_vendored`, `get_vendored_aliases`, `get_distutils_aliases`) and the function `pre_safe_import_module(api)` are the main integration points for hooks: they allow hooks to detect vendored copies and create alias modules so PyInstaller treats vendored packages as if they were top-level modules.

### Functions & Classes
- `_retrieve_setuptools_info` — module-level function, decorated with `@isolated.decorate`.
  - Purpose: Run inside an isolated subprocess to safely import and inspect `setuptools` and related packages without impacting the main process.
  - What it does / returned data:
    - Attempts `importlib.import_module("setuptools")`; returns `None` if not importable.
    - Uses `importlib_metadata.version("setuptools")` and `packaging.version.Version` to determine `version` as a tuple (the `.release` attribute from packaging.version.Version).
    - Determines `distutils_vendored` (bool) and collects `distutils_modules` when setuptools version >= (60, 0) by collecting submodules under `setuptools._distutils`.
    - Detects whether certain candidate packages are exposed via `setuptools._vendor` and sets entries in `vendored_status` (a dict mapping candidate package names to bool).
    - Collects `vendored_modules` by using `collect_submodules` on `setuptools._vendor` and `setuptools._vendor.jaraco` (with exclusions).
    - Collects `vendored_data` (data files) for vendored dependencies via `collect_data_files` when setuptools >= (71, 0).
    - Returns a dict with keys exactly:
      - "available" (True if setuptools import succeeded),
      - "version" (tuple from packaging.version.Version.release),
      - "distutils_vendored" (bool),
      - "distutils_modules" (list),
      - "vendored_status" (dict),
      - "vendored_modules" (list),
      - "vendored_data" (list).
  - Importance: This is the single place that analyzes the environment and produces the authoritative data that `SetuptoolsInfo` caches.

- `class SetuptoolsInfo` — class that lazily caches setuptools inspection data.
  - `__init__(self)` — constructor.
    - Currently empty (pass). Instance created at module import as `setuptools_info`.
  - `__repr__(self)` — returns the string "SetuptoolsInfo".
    - Makes logging messages readable.
  - `__getattr__(self, name)` — custom attribute access hook.
    - Purpose: Delay initialization until a property is first requested. If `available` already in `self.__dict__` and attribute not found, raise AttributeError. Otherwise call `_load_setuptools_info()` and then return the requested attribute via `getattr(self, name)`.
    - Importance: Enables lazy/cached initialization so hooks can cheaply import `setuptools_info` and only trigger the heavy subprocess inspection when they actually need attributes.
  - `_load_setuptools_info(self)` — performs initialization and caching.
    - Purpose: Calls `_retrieve_setuptools_info()` (in isolated subprocess) and copies returned values into attributes on the instance.
    - Initializes default safe attributes first:
      - `self.available = False`
      - `self.version = None`
      - `self.distutils_vendored = False`
      - `self.distutils_modules = []`
      - `self.vendored_status = dict()`
      - `self.vendored_modules = []`
      - `self.vendored_data = []`
    - If `_retrieve_setuptools_info()` raises or returns None, initialization stops (keeps defaults). Otherwise, copies keys from the returned dict to attributes on `self` (so the attribute names match the dict keys returned by `_retrieve_setuptools_info()`).
    - Importance: Ensures attributes exist even if setuptools isn't available or an error occurs, and caches the real values for subsequent access.
  - `is_vendored(self, module_name)` — returns `self.vendored_status.get(module_name, False)`.
    - Purpose: Check whether `module_name` (a top-level package name, e.g., "importlib_metadata" or "tomli") is a setuptools-vendored copy.
    - Importance: Primary convenience method used by hooks to detect vendored modules.
  - `_create_vendored_aliases(vendored_name, module_name, modules_list)` — `@staticmethod`.
    - Purpose: Given a vendored prefix (e.g., "setuptools._vendor.packagename"), a target alias base (e.g., "packagename" or "distutils"), and a list of discovered vendored module names (`modules_list`), create pairs (alias_name, vendored_module_name) for every vendored submodule whose name starts with `vendored_name`. The alias_name is constructed by replacing the vendored prefix with the desired module_name prefix.
    - Returns a generator of (alias_name, vendored_module_name) pairs.
    - Importance: Utility to create alias mappings used by get_vendored_aliases and get_distutils_aliases.
  - `get_vendored_aliases(self, module_name)` — returns aliases for a vendored package.
    - Purpose: For `module_name` (top-level name, e.g. "tomli"), constructs `vendored_name = f"setuptools._vendor.{module_name}"` and returns generator from `_create_vendored_aliases(vendored_name, module_name, self.vendored_modules)`.

  - Purpose: Public, lazily-initialized object that hooks import and read. Any attribute access triggers loading if not already loaded.
  - Importance: Primary entry point for other hooks and logic; the object documented in this file.

    - Uses `setuptools_info.get_vendored_aliases(module_name)` to iterate aliased_name, real_vendored_name pairs.
  - Importance: This is the convenience function called from hook files to create alias modules so PyInstaller treats vendored modules as the top-level package; directly relevant to handling setuptools-vendored dependencies at freeze time.

from PyInstaller.utils.hooks.setuptools import setuptools_info

hiddenimports = []

# From Python 3.6 and later ``distutils.sysconfig`` takes on the same behaviour as regular ``sysconfig`` of moving the
# config vars to a module (see hook-sysconfig.py). It doesn't use a nice `get module name` function like ``sysconfig``
# does to help us locate it but the module is the same file that ``sysconfig`` uses so we can use the
# ``_get_sysconfigdata_name()`` from regular ``sysconfig``.
try:
    import sysconfig
    hiddenimports += [sysconfig._get_sysconfigdata_name()]
except AttributeError:
    # Either sysconfig has no attribute _get_sysconfigdata_name (i.e., the function does not exist), or this is Windows
    # and the _get_sysconfigdata_name() call failed due to missing sys.abiflags attribute.
    pass

# Starting with setuptools 60.0, the vendored distutils overrides the stdlib one (which will be removed in python 3.12
# anyway), so check if we are using that version. While the distutils override behavior can be controleld via the
# ``SETUPTOOLS_USE_DISTUTILS`` environment variable, the latter may have a different value during the build and at the
# runtime, and so we need to ensure that both stdlib and setuptools variant of distutils are collected.
if setuptools_info.available and setuptools_info.version >= (60, 0):
    hiddenimports += ['setuptools._distutils']

This hook module for PyInstaller (hook-distutils.py) determines additional modules that must be included (hidden imports) when packaging applications that use distutils and its interactions with sysconfig and setuptools. It builds the module-level list `hiddenimports` based on the runtime environment and setuptools version.

### Architecture
- Module-level imports: imports `setuptools_info` from `PyInstaller.utils.hooks.setuptools` and conditionally imports the standard library `sysconfig`.
- Module-level list `hiddenimports` is initialized as an empty list and then extended in two places:
  - A try/except block that attempts to append the result of `sysconfig._get_sysconfigdata_name()` (for Python 3.6+ behavior).
  - A conditional block that appends the string `'setuptools._distutils'` when `setuptools_info` indicates setuptools >= 60.0.
- The file relies on runtime inspection (presence of attributes and setuptools version tuple) rather than defining classes or functions.

### Functions & Classes
This file defines no functions or classes. The important module-level names and calls are described below:

- `setuptools_info` — imported from `PyInstaller.utils.hooks.setuptools`. It is used to detect whether setuptools is available (`setuptools_info.available`) and the installed setuptools version (`setuptools_info.version`). This detection controls whether the vendored setuptools distutils (`'setuptools._distutils'`) must be added to `hiddenimports`. Its correctness is crucial for ensuring both stdlib and setuptools-provided distutils are collected.

- `hiddenimports` — module-level list initialized as `[]`. This is the main output of the hook: PyInstaller reads this to know extra modules to include. The file mutates this list by appending:
  - the sysconfig data module name (when available), and
  - `'setuptools._distutils'` (when setuptools >= 60.0).

- `sysconfig` — standard library module imported in a try block. The file relies on it to access the function:
  - `sysconfig._get_sysconfigdata_name()` — called and its return value appended to `hiddenimports`. This call is important because, starting with Python 3.6, `distutils.sysconfig` moves config vars to a module: obtaining that module name ensures the module used by `distutils.sysconfig` is included in the frozen bundle. The code expects `_get_sysconfigdata_name` to exist; if not, it falls back silently.

- `AttributeError` (exception used in the except block) — caught if `sysconfig` does not have `_get_sysconfigdata_name` or if a call to `_get_sysconfigdata_name()` fails (the comment also mentions a Windows-specific failure due to missing `sys.abiflags`). The except block contains `pass`, meaning missing attribute or failure is tolerated and no hidden import is added in that case.

- `'setuptools._distutils'` (string literal) — appended to `hiddenimports` when `setuptools_info.available and setuptools_info.version >= (60, 0)`. This ensures the vendored distutils provided by recent setuptools releases is included along with (or instead of) the stdlib distutils.

- `distutils.sysconfig` — mentioned in comments; the hook’s purpose is to cover the behavior of `distutils.sysconfig` as described in `hook-sysconfig.py`.
- `hook-sysconfig.py` — referenced for related behavior; not used directly here but conceptually linked.

- The setuptools version check `if setuptools_info.available and setuptools_info.version >= (60, 0): hiddenimports += ['setuptools._distutils']` — modify the version threshold, add environment-variable-based checks, or include additional setuptools-provided modules. from PyInstaller.utils.hooks.setuptools import setuptools_info

hiddenimports = []

# From Python 3.6 and later ``distutils.sysconfig`` takes on the same behaviour as regular ``sysconfig`` of moving the
# config vars to a module (see hook-sysconfig.py). It doesn't use a nice `get module name` function like ``sysconfig``
# does to help us locate it but the module is the same file that ``sysconfig`` uses so we can use the
# ``_get_sysconfigdata_name()`` from regular ``sysconfig``.
try:
    import sysconfig
    hiddenimports += [sysconfig._get_sysconfigdata_name()]
except AttributeError:
    # Either sysconfig has no attribute _get_sysconfigdata_name (i.e., the function does not exist), or this is Windows
    # and the _get_sysconfigdata_name() call failed due to missing sys.abiflags attribute.
    pass

# Starting with setuptools 60.0, the vendored distutils overrides the stdlib one (which will be removed in python 3.12
# anyway), so check if we are using that version. While the distutils override behavior can be controleld via the
# ``SETUPTOOLS_USE_DISTUTILS`` environment variable, the latter may have a different value during the build and at the
# runtime, and so we need to ensure that both stdlib and setuptools variant of distutils are collected.
if setuptools_info.available and setuptools_info.version >= (60, 0):
    hiddenimports += ['setuptools._distutils']

from PyInstaller.utils.hooks.setuptools import setuptools_info

hiddenimports = []

# From Python 3.6 and later ``distutils.sysconfig`` takes on the same behaviour as regular ``sysconfig`` of moving the
# config vars to a module (see hook-sysconfig.py). It doesn't use a nice `get module name` function like ``sysconfig``
# does to help us locate it but the module is the same file that ``sysconfig`` uses so we can use the
# ``_get_sysconfigdata_name()`` from regular ``sysconfig``.
try:
    import sysconfig
    hiddenimports += [sysconfig._get_sysconfigdata_name()]
except AttributeError:
    # Either sysconfig has no attribute _get_sysconfigdata_name (i.e., the function does not exist), or this is Windows
    # and the _get_sysconfigdata_name() call failed due to missing sys.abiflags attribute.
    pass

# Starting with setuptools 60.0, the vendored distutils overrides the stdlib one (which will be removed in python 3.12
# anyway), so check if we are using that version. While the distutils override behavior can be controleld via the
# ``SETUPTOOLS_USE_DISTUTILS`` environment variable, the latter may have a different value during the build and at the
# runtime, and so we need to ensure that both stdlib and setuptools variant of distutils are collected.
if setuptools_info.available and setuptools_info.version >= (60, 0):
    hiddenimports += ['setuptools._distutils']

### File Overview
This hook configures how PyInstaller treats the Python stdlib module sysconfig when building frozen apps. It excludes macOS-specific helper modules on non-macOS platforms and attempts to include platform-specific sysconfig data modules when available.

### Architecture
- The module begins by importing the stdlib module `sys`.
- It uses a platform check against `sys.platform` to set a module-level variable `excludedimports`.
- It then tries to import `sysconfig` and, if possible, sets a module-level variable `hiddenimports` to include the platform-specific sysconfig-data module name returned by `sysconfig._get_sysconfigdata_name()`.
- A try/except catches `AttributeError` to avoid crashing when `_get_sysconfigdata_name` is absent or when calling it fails (notably on some Windows builds).

### Functions & Classes
This file contains no functions or classes. Relevant module-level names and statements:

- `sys` — the imported stdlib module used for platform detection (`sys.platform`).
  - Importance: Determines whether `_osx_support` should be excluded for non-darwin platforms.

- `if sys.platform != 'darwin':` (platform check) — conditionally executes the next statement.
  - Importance: Ensures macOS-specific module `_osx_support` is only considered on darwin (macOS) systems.

- `excludedimports = ["_osx_support"]` — module-level variable assigned when `sys.platform != 'darwin'`.
  - Importance: Tells PyInstaller to exclude `_osx_support` from dependency collection on non-macOS platforms. This prevents trying to bundle a macOS-only helper into builds for other platforms.

- `try:` / `except AttributeError:` block — attempts to import `sysconfig` and call `_get_sysconfigdata_name`.
  - Importance: Safely tries to discover and include the platform-specific `_sysconfigdata_*` module name, while avoiding failures on Python builds that lack the function or where the call raises `AttributeError`.

- `import sysconfig` — imports the stdlib `sysconfig` module inside the try block.
  - Importance: Needed to access `_get_sysconfigdata_name()` when present.

- `hiddenimports = [sysconfig._get_sysconfigdata_name()]` — assigns `hiddenimports` to a list containing the string returned by `_get_sysconfigdata_name()`.
  - Importance: Instructs PyInstaller to explicitly collect the platform-specific compiled sysconfig data module (e.g., `_sysconfigdata_m_linux_x86_64-linux-gnu`) which Python 3.6+ or some Anaconda builds may require at runtime.

- `except AttributeError: pass` — swallows `AttributeError`.
  - Importance: Handles two distinct cases described in comments:
    - The `sysconfig` module does not define `_get_sysconfigdata_name` (older Python versions).
    - On some Windows builds, calling `_get_sysconfigdata_name()` fails due to `sys` missing `abiflags`; in that case we should not crash the hook.

Additional important comments in the file (not code):
- References to CPython source lines for sysconfig behavior on specific Python versions.
- Notes that Python 3.6 introduced platform-specific `_sysconfigdata_*` modules and that some Anaconda builds backported this behavior.
- Note that on MSYS2/MINGW Python, calling `_get_sysconfigdata_name()` works and the returned module should be collected.

### Relevance to Agent's Goal
- The file directly handles how `_osx_support` is treated: it sets `excludedimports = ["_osx_support"]` when `sys.platform != 'darwin'`. That means `_osx_support` is left alone (not excluded) on macOS builds, which is crucial if your changes touch `_osx_support` interplay.
- The file also ensures that the platform-specific `_sysconfigdata_*` module is included when possible (`hiddenimports = [sysconfig._get_sysconfigdata_name()]`), and documents the Windows/MSYS2/MINGW edge cases where calling `_get_sysconfigdata_name()` may fail. If you plan modifications that affect sysconfig or `_osx_support`, pay attention to:
  - The conditional exclusion of `_osx_support` for non-darwin platforms.
  - The try/except behavior around `sysconfig._get_sysconfigdata_name()` and the Windows `sys.abiflags` issue.

output.pr_title: Fix for failing test_site_module_disabled on macOS and python 3.9
output.pr_body:
Over the last few days, the `test_site_module_disabled` started to fail on macOS python 3.9 runner (e.g., [here](https://github.com/pyinstaller/pyinstaller/actions/runs/14001775971/job/39212391324?pr=9067)).

The failure is caused by `site` module being already imported at the start of the test, which is explicitly checking that this is not the case (as `site` module should be disabled in the interpreter config, and thus not imported during its initialization). 

It turns out that this is caused by import of `setuptools` in the run-time hook for `setuptools`, but only with `setuptools` v77.0.3 that was released on Mar 20. The import chain is `pyi_rth_setuptools.py` -> `setuptools` -> `setuptools.dist` -> `setuptools.command` -> `setuptools._distutils.command` -> `setuptools._distutils.command.build_ext` -> `site`,
and was likely brought about by https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/commit/1d120d919bd6d5cb8ba1b179c2099bd947d2c466.

It also turns out that the failing test under python 3.9 on macOS is collecting `setuptools`, while the (succeeding) tests under other versions (and OSes) are not. The offending import chain turns out to be: `pyi_site_module_disabled.py` -> `site` -> `_sitebuiltins` -> `pydoc` -> `sysconfig` -> `_osx_support` -> `distutils` (-> `setuptools`).

This is caused by a [conditional import in `_osx_support.compiler_fixup`](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/f3994ade31a563d49806cf6a681d1b1115fccaa3/Lib/_osx_support.py#L430-L434), which itself seems to be intended to be used by `distutils`.

This explains why we are seeing this only on macOS, and only with python 3.9 - latest `setuptools` do not  support python 3.8 anymore, and the use of `distutils.log` in `_osx_support.compiler_fixup` was removed in python >= 3.10.

Therefore, add a hook for `_osx_support` that excludes `distutils`. In addition to fixing the failing test, this should also ensure that `distutils` / `setuptools` do no end up pulled into basic applications that import either `site` or `sysconfig` (or a module that imports either of these) when building with python 3.8 or 3.9 on macOs.

Explore the source code to identify the files and functions to modify.
output.masca_optimized:
Summary of relevant MASCA analysis tailored to this PR:

- Motivation and root cause: Tests failing because the `site` module is already imported prior to test start. On macOS + CPython 3.9, importing `site` can transitively import `_osx_support`, which conditionally imports `distutils` (and, via setuptools shim, `setuptools`), pulling in modules that should not be present when site is disabled. Additionally, the setuptools runtime hook (pyi_rth_setuptools.py) imports setuptools and may install its distutils shim; with newer setuptools this can trigger `site` to be imported via distutils internals.

- Attack surface in codebase: PyInstaller hook files are responsible for telling analysis which modules to include or exclude. The offending chain involves `_osx_support` importing `distutils`. Therefore, adding a hook for `_osx_support` to exclude `distutils` will prevent the static analysis from adding `distutils` as a dependency of `_osx_support` and thus prevent downstream inclusion of `setuptools` in basic apps that only import `site` or `sysconfig`.

- Minimal safe fix: Add a new PyInstaller hook file named hook-_osx_support.py that sets excludedimports = ['distutils']. This instructs PyInstaller's analysis to treat distutils as a deliberately excluded import when analyzing `_osx_support`.

- Why minimal fix works: `_osx_support` only conditionally imports `distutils` to support compiler fixups on macOS. Excluding `distutils` for `_osx_support` prevents packaging tools from collecting distutils/setuptools due to that conditional import. This is safe because most applications that genuinely need distutils will explicitly import it or otherwise cause it to be included; we only suppress the spurious inclusion caused by `_osx_support` importing distutils as an internal helper.

- Other considerations & alternatives:
  - We could also add handling in hook-distutils.py to explicitly manage 'distutils._osx_support' or 'setuptools._distutils._osx_support' as hiddenimports. However, the reported problem is distutils being pulled in transitively; excluding the import in the `_osx_support` hook is more direct and minimal.
  - We might consider excluding 'setuptools' or 'setuptools._distutils' as well in some cases, but that risks hiding legitimate uses. The current fix targets the shorter, well-scoped problem: avoid distutils being pulled from `_osx_support` during analysis.

- Risk assessment: Low. The hook change only affects static analysis for module `_osx_support`. On macOS, `_osx_support` is platform-specific; excluding distutils as a hidden import for that module prevents spurious inclusion. If a frozen app truly requires distutils, it will still be included if the app explicitly imports it. Tests should confirm no regression: run the existing test suite (notably test_site_module_disabled) on macOS/py3.9 and other platforms/versions.

- Files to add/modify: Create PyInstaller/hooks/hook-_osx_support.py with excludedimports = ['distutils'].

- Functions to change: None (this is a new hook file without runtime functions).

This prevents _osx_support's conditional import of distutils from causing distutils (and via setuptools shim, setuptools) to be collected when analyzing modules like site/sysconfig on macOS (fixes test_site_module_disabled on macOS + CPython 3.9).

output.analysis_summary:
Analysis and proposed change

What fails and why:
- The failing test (test_site_module_disabled) asserts that 'site' should not already be imported. On macOS + Python 3.9 the analysis/collection process can collect setuptools/distutils due to import chains that go through _osx_support. That causes site to be imported earlier than the test expects.

Root cause:
- A conditional import inside CPython's Lib/_osx_support.py (compiler_fixup) imports distutils (and via setuptools' distutils shim, possibly setuptools). When PyInstaller analyzes modules such as site or sysconfig it may traverse into _osx_support and discover that it imports distutils, thus collecting distutils and possibly setuptools into frozen apps that don't otherwise need them.

Fix implemented:
- Add a PyInstaller hook file named hook-_osx_support.py that sets excludedimports = ['distutils'] to stop the static analysis from treating `_osx_support`'s conditional import of distutils as a required dependency.

Why this is minimal and safe:
- It targets only the problematic conditional import at analysis time. Applications that legitimately need distutils will still include it by explicit import. This prevents accidental inclusion of distutils/setuptools caused by _osx_support and should fix the failing test on macOS + Python 3.9.

Where to look and verify:
- New hook: PyInstaller/hooks/hook-_osx_support.py should be created.
- Run the test suite (notably tests/functional/test_site_module_disabled) on macOS/py3.9 to verify the failure is resolved.

Optional: if future corner cases appear where setuptools._distutils or vendored distutils ends up being collected via other paths, consider enhancing hook-distutils.py to explicitly handle 'distutils._osx_support' or 'setuptools._distutils._osx_support' as hiddenimports. For the immediate failing test, adding the exclusion on _osx_support is the correct minimal intervention.

## PR Title
Fix for failing test_site_module_disabled on macOS and python 3.9

## PR Body
Over the last few days, the `test_site_module_disabled` started to fail on macOS python 3.9 runner (e.g., [here](https://github.com/pyinstaller/pyinstaller/actions/runs/14001775971/job/39212391324?pr=9067)).

The failure is caused by `site` module being already imported at the start of the test, which is explicitly checking that this is not the case (as `site` module should be disabled in the interpreter config, and thus not imported during its initialization). 

It turns out that this is caused by import of `setuptools` in the run-time hook for `setuptools`, but only with `setuptools` v77.0.3 that was released on Mar 20. The import chain is `pyi_rth_setuptools.py` -> `setuptools` -> `setuptools.dist` -> `setuptools.command` -> `setuptools._distutils.command` -> `setuptools._distutils.command.build_ext` -> `site`,
and was likely brought about by https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/commit/1d120d919bd6d5cb8ba1b179c2099bd947d2c466.

It also turns out that the failing test under python 3.9 on macOS is collecting `setuptools`, while the (succeeding) tests under other versions (and OSes) are not. The offending import chain turns out to be: `pyi_site_module_disabled.py` -> `site` -> `_sitebuiltins` -> `pydoc` -> `sysconfig` -> `_osx_support` -> `distutils` (-> `setuptools`).

This is caused by a [conditional import in `_osx_support.compiler_fixup`](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/f3994ade31a563d49806cf6a681d1b1115fccaa3/Lib/_osx_support.py#L430-L434), which itself seems to be intended to be used by `distutils`.

This explains why we are seeing this only on macOS, and only with python 3.9 - latest `setuptools` do not  support python 3.8 anymore, and the use of `distutils.log` in `_osx_support.compiler_fixup` was removed in python >= 3.10.

Therefore, add a hook for `_osx_support` that excludes `distutils`. In addition to fixing the failing test, this should also ensure that `distutils` / `setuptools` do no end up pulled into basic applications that import either `site` or `sysconfig` (or a module that imports either of these) when building with python 3.8 or 3.9 on macOs.

Explore the source code to identify the files and functions to modify.

## MASCA Analysis (optimized)
Summary of relevant MASCA analysis tailored to this PR:

- Motivation and root cause: Tests failing because the `site` module is already imported prior to test start. On macOS + CPython 3.9, importing `site` can transitively import `_osx_support`, which conditionally imports `distutils` (and, via setuptools shim, `setuptools`), pulling in modules that should not be present when site is disabled. Additionally, the setuptools runtime hook (pyi_rth_setuptools.py) imports setuptools and may install its distutils shim; with newer setuptools this can trigger `site` to be imported via distutils internals.

- Attack surface in codebase: PyInstaller hook files are responsible for telling analysis which modules to include or exclude. The offending chain involves `_osx_support` importing `distutils`. Therefore, adding a hook for `_osx_support` to exclude `distutils` will prevent the static analysis from adding `distutils` as a dependency of `_osx_support` and thus prevent downstream inclusion of `setuptools` in basic apps that only import `site` or `sysconfig`.

- Minimal safe fix: Add a new PyInstaller hook file named hook-_osx_support.py that sets excludedimports = ['distutils']. This instructs PyInstaller's analysis to treat distutils as a deliberately excluded import when analyzing `_osx_support`.

- Why minimal fix works: `_osx_support` only conditionally imports `distutils` to support compiler fixups on macOS. Excluding `distutils` for `_osx_support` prevents packaging tools from collecting distutils/setuptools due to that conditional import. This is safe because most applications that genuinely need distutils will explicitly import it or otherwise cause it to be included; we only suppress the spurious inclusion caused by `_osx_support` importing distutils as an internal helper.

- Other considerations & alternatives:
  - We could also add handling in hook-distutils.py to explicitly manage 'distutils._osx_support' or 'setuptools._distutils._osx_support' as hiddenimports. However, the reported problem is distutils being pulled in transitively; excluding the import in the `_osx_support` hook is more direct and minimal.
  - We might consider excluding 'setuptools' or 'setuptools._distutils' as well in some cases, but that risks hiding legitimate uses. The current fix targets the shorter, well-scoped problem: avoid distutils being pulled from `_osx_support` during analysis.

- Risk assessment: Low. The hook change only affects static analysis for module `_osx_support`. On macOS, `_osx_support` is platform-specific; excluding distutils as a hidden import for that module prevents spurious inclusion. If a frozen app truly requires distutils, it will still be included if the app explicitly imports it. Tests should confirm no regression: run the existing test suite (notably test_site_module_disabled) on macOS/py3.9 and other platforms/versions.

- Files to add/modify: Create PyInstaller/hooks/hook-_osx_support.py with excludedimports = ['distutils'].

- Functions to change: None (this is a new hook file without runtime functions).

## Files to Modify
- PyInstaller/hooks/hook-_osx_support.py: Add a new hook for the stdlib module _osx_support that excludes 'distutils' from analysis. This prevents _osx_support's conditional import of distutils from causing distutils (and via setuptools shim, setuptools) to be collected when analyzing modules like site/sysconfig on macOS (fixes test_site_module_disabled on macOS + CPython 3.9).

## Analysis Summary
Analysis and proposed change

What fails and why:
- The failing test (test_site_module_disabled) asserts that 'site' should not already be imported. On macOS + Python 3.9 the analysis/collection process can collect setuptools/distutils due to import chains that go through _osx_support. That causes site to be imported earlier than the test expects.

Root cause:
- A conditional import inside CPython's Lib/_osx_support.py (compiler_fixup) imports distutils (and via setuptools' distutils shim, possibly setuptools). When PyInstaller analyzes modules such as site or sysconfig it may traverse into _osx_support and discover that it imports distutils, thus collecting distutils and possibly setuptools into frozen apps that don't otherwise need them.

Fix implemented:
- Add a PyInstaller hook file named hook-_osx_support.py that sets excludedimports = ['distutils'] to stop the static analysis from treating `_osx_support`'s conditional import of distutils as a required dependency.

Why this is minimal and safe:
- It targets only the problematic conditional import at analysis time. Applications that legitimately need distutils will still include it by explicit import. This prevents accidental inclusion of distutils/setuptools caused by _osx_support and should fix the failing test on macOS + Python 3.9.

Where to look and verify:
- New hook: PyInstaller/hooks/hook-_osx_support.py should be created.
- Run the test suite (notably tests/functional/test_site_module_disabled) on macOS/py3.9 to verify the failure is resolved.

Optional: if future corner cases appear where setuptools._distutils or vendored distutils ends up being collected via other paths, consider enhancing hook-distutils.py to explicitly handle 'distutils._osx_support' or 'setuptools._distutils._osx_support' as hiddenimports.
